transcript:
[doctor] patient shirley taylor . date of birth 5/4/1961 . mrn 0000123 . patient is a 60 year old right-handed female new patient referred by dr. jacob ward for evaluation of bilateral hand numbness and tingling . please note that i personally reviewed the ct brain with and without contrasting did 2/5/21 . uh , no metastatic disease . no acute intercranial abnormalities .
[doctor] hello ms. taylor . how are you doing this morning ?
[patient] hi dr . lopez . this weekend was a bit rough . i've had this numbness and tingling for a few weeks now .
[doctor] so , in both of your hands ?
[patient] yeah . ever since my car wreck last december when i broke them . well , once they heal , i mean , i noticed it more , the , the tingling and numbness .
[doctor] are you still wearing the splint only on your right wrist ?
[patient] no . i wear the left one also sometimes . the right one's just worse . the splint seems to help . both have gotten worse over the last month . when i broke them though , my wrist was definitely in worse shape than it is today . i've been having some mild stiffness in my neck too . i hit my head during the accident , and they told me it was a contusion .
[doctor] okay . and where exactly on the right side would you say the tingling and numbness is ?
[patient] on the right , i can really notice it from my middle and ring finger all the way up to my forearm , and on my left , a bit more the pinky and the ring and not the middle .
[doctor] all right . and , um , is there is pain or , or just tingling and numbness ?
[patient] uh , mainly just sore . sometimes they throb , but overall , they feel weaker too , especially when i'm using them . the tingling happens some more , but when they hurt , you know , it goes away and then the tingling's just dull .
[doctor] what kind of activities ?
[patient] well , really , i guess , just more so at night , and then it just kind of comes and goes throughout the day .
[doctor] okay . and do you experience any loss of sensation in your hands or fingers ?
[patient] uh , like , like feeling something ? no , not that i can think of really . in general , they might be less sensitive than they should be , but i can still feel . they gave me some nerve pain medicine , but i have n't been taking it like i should be .
[doctor] okay . looks like your medical history is notable for chronic kidney disease , gout , hypertension , anxiety , gerd , and diabetes .
[patient] well , i had pre-diabetes , but i got in under control changing my diet .
[doctor] okay . good . and are you still on the carvedilol 25 milligrams two times a day for hypertension and lexapro 10 milligrams a day for anxiety ? looks like your allergic to sulfa drugs as well . is that correct ?
[patient] yeah , i'm still taking those and sulfa just causes me to break out in a rash .
[doctor] and no history of thyroid issues , right ?
[patient] nope .
[doctor] all right . thank you ms. taylor . i'll be right back for your exam . i want to look up that medication you mentioned for your nerve pain .
[patient] all right . thanks .
[doctor] review of systems , please make the following changes . uh , constitutional , patient reports fatigue and weight gain . gi , um , excessive appetite and bloating . eyes , floaters . uh , cardiovascular , shortness of breath and leg swelling . endocrine , positive diabetes , always hungry . skin , nail bed trenches , dryness , itching . musculoskeletal , gout , muscle pain . neurological , parasthesia , bilateral hands .
[doctor] um , please use the default normal neuro-physical exam with the following changes . sensations normal except pin prick sensation diminished in the third and forth fingers on the right . and the fourth and fifth fingers on the left . reflexes are one plus and symmetric , two plus knees , toes are down going bilaterally , tinel's sign positive at both wrists and both elbows .
[doctor] let's move onto assessment and plan , bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome , question of bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome . i discussed my impression with the patient , i will schedule her for emg , ncv . if her symptoms do not resolve , we can consider hand clinic referral for possible symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery although she can pursue these uh , through her current orthopedist . i encouraged her to wear her splints , returned for emg , ncv . end of note .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Upper extremity tingling

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

The patient is a 60-year-old female referred today for evaluation of numbness and tingling.

The patient complains of numbness, tingling, and pain in both hands which began last December after she broke both wrists in an MVA and which has progressively worsened since then. She continues to wear a splint on the right wrist all the time and on the left only intermittently, which she states is because the right wrist was injured more severely in the MVA. The sensation radiates from the hands into the forearms and is equal bilaterally. The 4th and 5th fingers in the left hand and 3rd and 4th on the right-hand tingle. She states that her fingers are sore with palpation and seem weaker than before. Any exertion exacerbates the tingling, and after something exacerbates the pain, the tingling seems "dull". The symptoms come and go and seem to occur mostly at night. She denies complete loss of sensation in any of her fingers, but states that they seem less sensitive than they should be. she reports mild stiffness in her neck. She notes that she did hit her head during the MVA and was diagnosed with a contusion. She states that she has been given "nerve pain medicine" but has not been taking it.

The patient takes carvedilol for her hypertension and Lexapro for her anxiety. She reports a history of prediabetes, which she has brought under control via dietary changes. She denies any history of thyroid issues.

REVIEW OF SYMPTOMS

 Constitutional Symptoms: The patient reports increased fatigue and weight gain.
 Eyes: Positive for floaters.
 Cardiovascular: Positive for leg swelling.
 Respiratory: Positive for shortness of breath.
 Gastrointestinal: Positive for excessive hunger and bloating.
 Musculoskeletal: Positive for gout and muscle pain.
 Integumentary: Positive for nail bed changes, dryness, and itching.
 Neurological: Positive for bilateral paresthesia of hands.
 Endocrine: Positive prediabetes and excessive hunger.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

Chronic kidney disease (CKD).
Prediabetes, diet controlled.
Hypertension.
Gastric esophageal reflux disorder (GERD)

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Carvedilol 25 mg twice daily.
Lexapro 10 mg daily.

ALLERGIES

Sulfa, causes rash.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological Exam
Sensation is normal except pinprick sensation diminished in the 3rd and 4th fingers on the right and the 4th and 5th fingers on the left. 1+ and symmetric, 2+ knees. Toes are downgoing bilaterally. Tinel's sign positive at both wrists and both elbows.

RESULTS

CT brain with and without contrast, 02/05/2021
Impression: No metastatic disease. No acute intracranial abnormalities. I personally reviewed the report and images.

ASSESSMENT

 Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, question of bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome.

PLAN

I discussed my impression with the patient. I will schedule her for EMG/NCV. If her symptoms do not resolve, we can consider Hand Clinic referral for possibly symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery, although she can also pursue these through her current orthopedist. I encouraged her to wear her splints.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return for EMG/NCV.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed female, presents with a 3-week history of bilateral hand numbness and tingling, which started after a car accident in December where she broke both wrists. She reports that the symptoms worsened over the last month, with the right wrist being worse than the left. She also mentions mild stiffness in her neck and a contusion to the head during the accident. She denies any pain, but describes the numbness and tingling as sore and throbbing, especially when using her hands. She reports that the symptoms are more pronounced at night and can come and go throughout the day. She denies any loss of sensation in her hands or fingers, but notes that they might be less sensitive than usual. She has been experiencing fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, and dryness, itching, and nail bed trenches on her skin. She has a history of chronic kidney disease, gout, hypertension, anxiety, GERD, and pre-diabetes, which she has managed with dietary changes. She is currently taking carvedilol 25 milligrams twice a day for hypertension, lexapro 10 milligrams a day for anxiety, and has a known allergy to sulfa drugs.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of chronic kidney disease, gout, hypertension, anxiety, GERD, and pre-diabetes, which she has managed with dietary changes.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, dryness, itching, and nail bed trenches on her skin, parasthesia, bilateral hands, gout, muscle pain, and diabetes.

Current medications: 
- Carvedilol 25 milligrams twice a day for hypertension
- Lexapro 10 milligrams a day for anxiety

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The physical exam was notable for diminished pin prick sensation in the third and fourth fingers on the right and the fourth and fifth fingers on the left. Reflexes were one plus and symmetric, with two plus knees, and down-going bilaterally. Tinel's sign was positive at both wrists and both elbows.

Test Results: 
- CT brain with and without contrast from 2/5/21: No metastatic disease, no acute intracranial abnormalities.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 60-year-old right-handed female with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome and possible bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome.

Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; Cubital tunnel syndrome; Chronic kidney disease; Gout; Hypertension; Anxiety; GERD; Pre-diabetes; Sulfa allergy.

Plan:
I discussed my impression with the patient and scheduled her for EMG and NCV. If her symptoms do not resolve, we can consider hand clinic referral for possible symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery, although she can pursue these through her current orthopedist. I encouraged her to wear her splints and return for EMG and NCV.

Follow-up: The patient will return for EMG and NCV, and if necessary, further management will be discussed.

Subjective:

She reports that the numbness and tingling have worsened over the last month. She also mentions mild stiffness in her neck, which she attributes to a contusion from the accident. The patient reports that the tingling and numbness are more pronounced on the right side, specifically in the middle and ring fingers and forearm, and on the left side, in the pinky and ring fingers. She describes the sensation as sore, with occasional throbbing and weakness, especially when using her hands. The patient reports that the symptoms are more pronounced at night and come and go throughout the day.

Past medical history: The patient has a notable medical history of chronic kidney disease, gout, hypertension, anxiety, GERD, and pre-diabetes, which she has controlled through dietary changes.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, floaters, nail bed trenches, dryness, itching, gout, muscle pain, and paresthesia in her bilateral hands.

Current medications: The patient is taking carvedilol 25 milligrams twice a day for hypertension, lexapro 10 milligrams a day for anxiety, and is allergic to sulfa drugs.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The physical exam is normal except for the following findings: pin prick sensation is diminished in the third and fourth fingers on the right and the fourth and fifth fingers on the left. Reflexes are one plus and symmetric, with two plus knees, and down-going toes bilaterally. Tinel's sign is positive at both wrists and both elbows.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List: Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome and a question of bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome. Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; Cubital tunnel syndrome; Chronic kidney disease; Gout; Hypertension; Anxiety; GERD; Pre-diabetes; Allergy to sulfa drugs.

Plan: I prescribed no medications, but I will schedule the patient for EMG and NCV to evaluate her symptoms. If her symptoms do not resolve, we can consider hand clinic referral for possible symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery. I encouraged her to wear her splints and return for EMG and NCV.

Follow-up: I scheduled the patient to return for EMG and NCV, and to discuss further management if necessary.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed female, presents with a 2-month history of bilateral hand numbness and tingling, which started after a car accident in December where she broke her wrists. She reports worsening symptoms over the last month, with tingling and numbness in both hands, particularly in the right hand from the middle and ring finger to the forearm, and on the left hand, more in the pinky and ring fingers. She experiences pain, soreness, and weakness in her hands, especially when using them, with the tingling becoming dull when pain subsides. She also reports mild stiffness in her neck. Past medical history: The patient has a history of chronic kidney disease, gout, hypertension, anxiety, GERD, and pre-diabetes, which is currently under control. Social history: The patient is allergic to sulfa drugs and has a history of a contusion to the head in the car accident.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, diabetes, and muscle pain. She also reports nail bed trenches, dryness, itching, and parasthesia in her bilateral hands.

Current medications: I prescribed carvedilol 25 milligrams two times a day for hypertension and Lexapro 10 milligrams a day for anxiety.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient's sensations are normal except for diminished pinprick sensation in the third and fourth fingers on the right and the fourth and fifth fingers on the left. Reflexes are one plus and symmetric, with two plus knees and down-going toes bilaterally. Tinel's sign is positive at both wrists and both elbows.

Test Results: Review of CT brain with and without contrast on 2/5/21 showed no metastatic disease or acute intracranial abnormalities.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome and a question of bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome.

Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; cubital tunnel syndrome; chronic kidney disease; gout; hypertension; anxiety; GERD; pre-diabetes; diabetes; allergy to sulfa drugs.

Plan:
I scheduled the patient for EMG and NCV to further evaluate her symptoms. If her symptoms do not resolve, I will consider referring her to a hand clinic for possible symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery. I encouraged her to wear her splints and return for EMG and NCV.

Follow-up: I will schedule a follow-up appointment for the patient to review the results of her EMG and NCV and determine the next course of action.
